message:
test,bench(frankenterm-core): add LabRuntime observation-loop tests and criterion benches (wa-1m7nk)

Complete the wa-1m7nk "observation loop" validation work under the
asupersync LabRuntime:

- crates/frankenterm-core/Cargo.toml: register new [[bench]] entry
  for observation_loop, feature-gated on asupersync-runtime
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs: add labruntime_observation
  test module (+453 lines) covering channel dispatch, multi-channel
  dispatch, adaptive sleep virtual-time advancement, and shutdown
  flag propagation; all gated on #[cfg(feature = "asupersync-runtime")]
- crates/frankenterm-core/benches/observation_loop.rs: new 217-line
  criterion benchmark suite measuring event dispatch latency,
  multi-channel select, sleep overhead, throughput, and RwLock
  contention under asupersync
- .beads/issues.jsonl: bead status bookkeeping reflecting wa-1m7nk
  progress

All additions — no existing tests removed or logic changed.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.2.3","title":"Migrate IPC, watch-daemon control sockets, and local control-plane streams to native Asupersync","description":"# Goal
Remove legacy async assumptions from the local transport layer that binds CLI, daemon, and watch-style control paths together.

This task exists because JSON-line IPC, local sockets, and related control streams are structural parts of the operator experience. They need native Asupersync ownership, not a migration seam that happens to pass tests today.","design":"# Design
1. Move accept loops, per-connection handlers, buffered reads and writes, and shutdown paths onto native Asupersync networking and structured concurrency.
2. Preserve protocol compatibility while changing the runtime semantics under the hood.
3. Make connection lifecycle and backpressure behavior deterministic and testable.
4. Fold any remaining compat use into explicit quarantine or remove it entirely.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Local control sockets and IPC code paths covered by this bead no longer depend on Tokio-like runtime helpers.
2. JSON-line protocol behavior remains stable while lifecycle semantics become native and structured.
3. Tests cover malformed input, concurrent clients, disconnects, and shutdown.
4. Remote verification is captured with exact commands and artifacts.
5. This bead follows `ft-xbnl0.1.4`'s shared verification contract: comprehensive unit coverage for touched code paths, at least one deterministic integration or E2E script with detailed timestamped logging and artifact capture, and exact `rch exec -- ...` verification commands recorded in the completion evidence.","notes":"This task is intentionally transport-focused. Higher-level command semantics belong to other beads.

Inventory cross-link (`ft-xbnl0.1.1`): this lane owns the local control-plane transport slice currently still reached through `runtime_compat`, specifically `crates/frankenterm-core/src/ipc.rs`, `native_events.rs`, `event_stream.rs`, and related local socket/control-stream helpers. Inherited blocker `wa-16hou` remains relevant context, but ProudSalmon already completed a bounded `ipc.rs` shutdown-semantics support slice on 2026-04-01, so this lane should build on that work rather than redoing it.

 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","title":"Replace TCP, TLS, and HTTP client or service boundaries with native Asupersync networking","description":"# Goal
Finish the network-facing runtime migration so service and client boundaries stop dragging Tokio-era dependencies into the system.

1. Replace direct TCP and TLS surfaces with native Asupersync networking where the project owns the boundary.
2. Replace outbound HTTP client usage with native Asupersync HTTP semantics.

4. Add tests and benchmarks for handshake behavior, concurrent connections, and request latency so the migration is not justified purely by ideology.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Covered TCP, TLS, and HTTP client or service surfaces no longer require direct Tokio-era networking crates.

4. Completion evidence records the exact remote commands and artifacts used to validate the cutover.
5.  {"id":"ft-xbnl0.2.5","title":"Collapse runtime_compat quarantine and remove dual-runtime feature seams from core APIs","description":"# Goal
Shrink the compatibility seam down to what is genuinely unavoidable and remove dual-runtime logic from core project-owned APIs.

This task exists because a repo can claim Asupersync while still keeping Tokio-shaped abstractions alive in `runtime_compat`, feature flags, and helper APIs. That is migration debt, not a finished architecture.","design":"# Design
1. Identify which compat exports are still used by project-owned core logic after the preceding migration tasks.
2. Replace those uses with native Asupersync primitives or move them behind explicit quarantine adapters.
3. Remove dead feature branches and comments that describe a dual-runtime future that is no longer intended.
4. Leave only deliberately isolated boundaries, each with a named owner and removal path if it still exists.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Core project-owned APIs no longer rely on `runtime_compat` as a long-term abstraction layer.
2. Dual-runtime feature seams are removed from covered surfaces.

4. The codebase narrative now matches the intended native-Asupersync architecture.
5. This bead follows `ft-xbnl0.1.4`'s shared verification contract: comprehensive unit coverage for touched code paths, at least one deterministic integration or E2E script with detailed timestamped logging and artifact capture, and exact `rch exec -- ...` verification commands recorded in the completion evidence.","notes":"This task is the architectural cleanup step that makes the migration real instead of forever in progress.

Turn the no-Tokio and no-Smol intent into a durable invariant.

This task exists because migration work is not closed until the repo actively prevents regression. Without hard gates, future contributions will quietly reintroduce runtime debt and undo the finish-line push.","design":"# Design
1. Add machine checks that fail when direct `tokio`, `smol`, or `async-io` usage returns in supported surfaces.
2. Extend doctor or audit surfaces so contributors can diagnose violations locally.
3. Sweep docs and comments so the narrative matches the final architecture.
4. Make the gate broad enough to defend shipping paths, tests, benches, and key docs without blocking legitimate quarantine that is explicitly owned.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Supported builds, tests, benches, and core docs are guarded against direct `tokio`, `smol`, and `async-io` reintroduction.

4. This bead provides the durable no-regression gate for the async migration lane.
5. This bead follows `ft-xbnl0.1.4`'s shared verification contract: comprehensive unit coverage for touched code paths, at least one deterministic integration or E2E script with detailed timestamped logging and artifact capture, and exact `rch exec -- ...` verification commands recorded in the completion evidence.","notes":"This is the enforcement step that turns the migration into a maintained property rather than a one-time cleanup.

Inventory cross-link (`ft-xbnl0.1.1`): this lane owns the permanent no-regression gate for the async migration, including manifest checks, source/doc sweeps, and explicit exemption handling for any named quarantine left after `ft-xbnl0.2.5`. The inherited final-cleanup bead is `wa-2h1ha`.

 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.3","title":"[EPIC] Supported-path capability completion and placeholder closure","description":"# Goal
Finish or honestly narrow every supported-path capability that still behaves like a placeholder, partial implementation, or misleading promise.

This epic exists because a swarm operator cannot trust a terminal or control-plane whose advertised pane lifecycle, alt-screen truth, scrollback visibility, detach semantics, renderer support, or mux-server operations still hide `todo!()` branches or fake state.","design":"# Design
1. Reuse the placeholder-remediation program where it already owns the surface.
2. Add explicit new tasks for gaps that are real but not yet represented cleanly, especially mux-server sessionhandler operations and any final supported-path sweep.
3. Prefer small honest capability surfaces over broad imaginary ones: either ship the behavior for real or narrow the contract in code, tests, and docs.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Every supported-path capability covered by this epic is either implemented for real or explicitly narrowed with operator-visible semantics.
2. Safety-relevant state such as alt-screen, detach support, pane lifecycle, and scrollback availability is no longer based on placeholders.
3. Renderer and platform support claims match actual tested behavior.
4. Shared mux-server implementation no longer contains supported-path `unimplemented!()` branches for pane and window operations.","notes":"This epic is about truth. If a feature is intentionally unsupported, say so explicitly and prove callers can distinguish that from not implemented yet.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:09:52.194726Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-03T17:41:00.157989Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["capability-truth","goal-line","placeholder-remediation"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.3","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:09:52.194726Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

4. Verify that robot-facing semantics stay coherent after the implementation changes.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Client-domain pane lifecycle and alt-screen truth are authoritative in the covered machine-control paths.

 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.3.2","title":"Finish live scrollback, pane state, and FTUI surfaces so operator interfaces read real data","description":"# Goal
Make operator-facing state and FTUI surfaces read live, real data rather than null adapters or placeholder views.

This task exists because scrollback visibility and FTUI state are not secondary luxuries in a swarm terminal. They are part of how operators debug agents, recover sessions, and trust what the system is telling them.","design":"# Design
1. Build on the real scrollback wiring and FTUI placeholder-remediation work already underway.
2. Ensure live pane data, historical data, and operator views all share authoritative sources instead of separate placeholder shims.
3. Keep the resulting surface bounded and explicit for large-scrollback future work.
4. Verify both data correctness and operator-visible behavior.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Covered scrollback and FTUI surfaces no longer rely on null adapters or placeholder view bodies.
2. Operator-visible state reflects live and historical data from authoritative sources.
3. The resulting surface is documented well enough that future large-scrollback work can build on it without guesswork.
4. Verification covers data fidelity, empty states, error states, and user-facing behavior.
5.
